summary: Fowler explores the fuzzy boundary between domain-specific languages (DSLs) and other constructs like APIs and general-purpose languages. He argues that DSLs are defined by their limited scope and capability, targeting a specific domain rather than general computing. For internal DSLs, the distinction from APIs is largely about feel and readability rather than technical difference. For external DSLs, Turing completeness and abstraction facilities often serve as distinguishing markers, though exceptions exist. Ultimately, Fowler concludes that intention—both from designers and users—matters more than rigid definitions, and usefulness should be the primary concern.
key_points: Internal DSLs are fundamentally APIs with a different feel, emphasizing readability and domain-focused syntax
External DSLs typically lack Turing completeness or abstraction facilities, distinguishing them from general-purpose languages
The boundary between DSL and GPL can depend on user intention—using XSLT for XML transforms treats it as DSL, solving eight queens treats it as GPL
Language designer intent matters: if creators envision limited domain focus, it's a DSL regardless of what clever users do with it
Practical usefulness matters more than definitional debates about what qualifies as a DSL
money_quotes: DSLs are limited both in scope (they refer to a particular domain) and capability (they lack features that are basic for general purpose languages).
An internal DSL is just an API with a fancy name (as the old Bell labs saying goes: 'library design is language design').
Thinking in DSL terms makes you think about readability in a different way, exploiting the syntax of the host language to create something that seems to stand on its own.
If I use XSLT only to transform an XML document then I'm treating it as a DSL - even if I do some fancy call out to embed some value in the output document.
This is one of those discussions are interesting ones to have in the pub but shouldn't detract from the good ideas in using DSLs.
DSLs should be designed and used to be a limited language tightly focused on a single problem.
And after all it's usefulness that really counts.
key_insight: The distinction between DSLs and other languages is less about technical boundaries and more about intention and usefulness—design for focused, limited scope and the classification takes care of itself.
